meta_title: For Students: client workflow to Solve no project ideas (Intermediate Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to client workflow and overcome no project ideas. A Intermediate-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: Client Workflow to Solve No Project Ideas
content.seo_description: Learn how to use a professional client workflow to overcome the 'no project ideas' block. A practical, intermediate-level guide using Antigravity and Claude Code.
content.answer_block: Stop chasing abstract ideas. The most effective way to build a portfolio is to solve real-world problems through a structured client workflow. By interviewing stakeholders, mapping pain points, and automating their manual tasks, you gain clear project requirements, guaranteed utility, and a high-value case study for your future career.
content.intro:
You’re staring at a blank IDE, the cursor blinking in a rhythm that feels less like a prompt and more like a taunt. You’ve mastered the tutorials, but building 'another to-do list app' feels like a dead end for your resume. The silence of your portfolio is deafening, and the uncertainty of what to build next is keeping you stagnant.

But here is the secret the pros won't tell you: you don't need a stroke of genius; you need a client. By shifting from 'what should I code' to 'who can I help,' you transform your development process from a guessing game into a high-impact engineering mission. Let’s walk through the exact workflow that turns professional pain points into your next flagship project.

steps.title: Identify the 'Broken' Workflow
steps.description: Find a local business or student group struggling with manual data entry or fragmented communication. Your goal is to find one repeatable process that takes them over an hour daily.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Act as a senior consultant. Create a 5-question interview template to uncover manual bottlenecks in small business operations. Focus on data silos and repetitive tasks.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Generate a template for a discovery call to identify technical debt in a local business workflow.'

steps.title: Define the MVP Scope
steps.description: Don't build a platform; build a utility. Use the interview data to define a single, high-impact feature that solves the primary friction point identified in step one.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Based on the identified bottleneck, scope a Minimum Viable Product that focuses exclusively on automating the data transfer between two platforms. Keep it to one core function.
steps.claude_code_command: claude terminal 'Create a project requirements document (PRD) for a micro-SaaS tool that automates [Insert Workflow].'

steps.title: Architect the Logic
steps.description: Map the data flow. Before writing code, ensure you understand where the data originates and where it needs to end up, including necessary validation layers.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Design a clean, modular architecture for a Node.js microservice that handles API authentication, data transformation, and error logging for this specific workflow.

steps.title: Bootstrap with Claude Code
steps.description: Use Claude Code to scaffold the boilerplate. This allows you to focus on the high-level logic rather than tedious configuration setup.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a directory structure and initial configuration files for a robust, production-ready TypeScript project, including testing and linting.

steps.title: Iterative Implementation
steps.description: Build the core logic in small, testable chunks. Use the client's feedback loop to refine the UI/UX as you integrate the automation.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write the core function for the API integration, including robust error handling and a simple retry mechanism for failed requests.
steps.claude_code_command: claude write 'Implement the API service layer with error handling for 4xx and 5xx status codes.'

pro_tips: Always build for a 'client of one'—solving a specific problem for one real person is worth more than a generic app for a thousand hypothetical users.
Focus on data portability; if your tool helps a user move data between two platforms, it becomes an essential part of their infrastructure.
Implement logging before you implement features; knowing exactly where a process fails is the difference between a junior and senior developer.
Use environment variables for everything; never hardcode an API key, even in your local prototype.
Ship the 'ugly' version first; if it solves the problem, the user will forgive the lack of polish, and you can iterate based on real usage.
content.next_steps: Once the MVP is functional, deploy it using a free tier service like Vercel or Railway. Document your findings in a GitHub README that highlights the 'Before vs. After' impact on the client's workflow to attract future employers.
content.conversion_block: You now have the framework to stop guessing and start engineering. This is how you move from a student to a professional developer. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
